A researcher presents a method for finding explanations. They first say brute-force iteration is impossible: the space is too large, so exhaustively searching candidates cannot work. They then propose a layered filtering pipeline. But if the target is a rare explanatory structure, the pipeline still requires a supply of candidates rich enough that at least one such structure appears before filtering eliminates it. So the impossibility claim does not disappear; it relocates. The bottleneck becomes candidate generation under rarity.

They defend the method by saying it uses minimal assumptions. But this standard is unstable. What counts as “minimal” depends on the framework from which one judges. Some explanations once seemed assumption-heavy and later looked simple when a new theory reorganized them. So the researcher cannot treat present-day simplicity as an objective final criterion. Their filter for “minimal assumptions” may reject exactly the kind of explanation that would later be seen as elegant.

A second defense is linguistic: the system does not need new words, only new combinations of old ones. But that fact alone proves little. Recombination can produce outputs that sound fresh while remaining paraphrases of patterns already implicit in prior material. Therefore the absence of new vocabulary cannot establish that genuinely new explanations are being produced. It shows only that expression is possible within an existing lexicon.

Now include the remaining axioms: “Selfsufficiency sibbing pizazzes antilaborist overbids” and “Ectoplastic millrind lagers overstretches ironmaking.” These strings must be treated as true sentences, but within the given premises they come without interpretation. They are examples of grammatical-looking combinations of existing words that carry no supplied explanatory role. Their truth, as axioms, does not make them useful explanations. Instead they sharpen the earlier point: recombination alone can yield well-formed sequences whose status as explanation is undetermined. If a pipeline admits such strings as candidates, then filtering must distinguish explanatory novelty from mere combinatorial novelty. If it excludes them, it needs criteria stronger than surface fluency or lexical reuse.

So the world implied by these premises is one in which explanation-finding faces a double uncertainty. Search cannot simply be dismissed, because filtering presupposes enough generation to encounter rare structures. And evaluation cannot rely securely on current judgments of simplicity or on the mere reuse of old words. The two opaque but true sentences mark the boundary condition: truth and explainability are not guaranteed by familiar components arranged into novel forms. The researcher’s method may still work, but these premises do not let it claim that rarity, simplicity, and recombination have been resolved rather than renamed.
